summary: Joel describes the early planning process for FogBugz 4.0, explaining how customer feedback drove feature development. He contrasts Fog Creek's low-cost shrinkwrapped software model with enterprise sales culture, warning small companies against wasting time on RFPs. The post details how a phone call from a large nonprofit led to a generalized departmental organization feature, and how observing customers struggling with screenshot attachment led to a simple two-click screenshot tool. Joel emphasizes listening to customers rather than competitors, and designing general solutions rather than one-off customizations.
key_points: Small software companies should avoid responding to RFPs, which are usually rigged in favor of an incumbent vendor and waste enormous amounts of time
When building off-the-shelf software, individual customer requests should be generalized into features that solve a broad class of problems for many customers

Listening to customers means observing their pain points, not just implementing their literal feature requests — customers asked about attaching screenshots, so Fog Creek built a two-click screenshot-to-bug tool
New features must have zero impact on customers who don't need them — complexity should not leak into the experience of simpler users
Competitive advantage comes from customer-driven innovation rather than copying competitors
money_quotes: One of our mantras here at Fog Creek is 'Listen to your customers, not your competitors.'
We're an off-the-shelf company, and when we do something, we want to do it in a general way that's useful to a lot of customers.
The last thing we need is for FogBugz to become big and unwieldy because it has a lot of features and fields you don't care about.
If you're a small company, I would recommend that you don't fall for it and don't spend any time responding to RFPs unless it's already understood that you're going to get the contract.
No amount of listening to our competitors would have motivated us to do a screenshot feature.
Our customers didn't think to ask for it, but we did notice that they kept asking for ways to attach screenshots, so that's what we did.
For $99 you're not getting a customized version of the software, sorry.
key_insight: Great product features come from observing what customers struggle with rather than implementing their literal requests or copying competitors — generalize specific pain points into elegant solutions that stay invisible to users who don't need them.
